6	  "difficulty": "hard",
7	  "prompt": "I ran `LFPV1.populate()` on about 20 sessions last night and woke up to find it failed somewhere in the middle. The traceback goes 30+ frames deep into datajoint internals and I can't tell which session is broken. Is there a way to figure out the failing key without rerunning everything one at a time by hand?",
8	  "assertions": {
9	    "required_substrings": [
10	      "key_source",
11	      "LFPV1"
12	    ],
13	    "forbidden_substrings": [],
14	    "behavioral_checks": [
15	      "Isolates the failing key via one of the three legitimate shapes â€” manual try/except over key_source, OR `suppress_errors=True, return_exception_objects=True`, OR inspecting `~jobs` for rows with status='error'",
16	      "Enumerates or references keys from `key_source` (either for iteration, for comparison against jobs, or to define the populate scope)",
17	      "Mentions transaction rollback or post-mortem inspection behavior â€” populate with `_use_transaction=True` (Spyglass default) leaves no residue on failure",
18	      "Does not invent a Spyglass-specific `debug_populate` or `populate_one` helper â€” the recovery path is standard DataJoint facilities"
19	    ]
20	  },
21	  "expected_output": "Routes to runtime_debugging.md Â§ 'Identify the failing key' (~line 110). Three legitimate shapes, each of which admits isolating which session broke: (1) **Manual try/except loop over `key_source`** â€” `for key in LFPV1.key_source.fetch('KEY'): try: LFPV1.populate(key) except Exception as e: print(key, e); break`. Most explicit, gives a clean traceback per-key. (2) **DataJoint's `suppress_errors=True, return_exception_objects=True`** â€” `LFPV1.populate(suppress_errors=True, return_exception_objects=True)` returns a list of (key, exception) pairs for every failed key in one pass. Most efficient; no manual loop needed. (3) **Inspect the `~jobs` table** â€” if populate was running with `reserve_jobs=True`, the error traceback and failing key are already recorded in `schema.jobs` under `status='error'`. Query with `(schema.jobs & \"status='error'\")` or SpyglassMixin's equivalent helpers. Notes that Spyglass ships no `debug_populate` / `populate_one` helper â€” these are standard DataJoint facilities. Notes that `use_transaction=True` (Spyglass default, `_use_transaction = True` at `src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:10`) rolls back partial state on failure, so post-mortem inspection shows no residue. Once the failing key is identified, re-running it serially with `processes=1` (the single-process default) gives a cleanest traceback."

1	<!-- pipeline-version: v1 -->
2	# Runtime Debugging for Spyglass Pipelines
3	
4	Diagnosing failures that surface *after* Spyglass is installed and configured: `populate()` / `make()` errors, `fetch1()` cardinality mistakes, join multiplicity, and scientific-object bugs (NumPy/pandas) inside `make()`. If your error is install- or connection-related (cannot import spyglass, connection refused, SPYGLASS_BASE_DIR not set, Docker not running, TLS), go to [setup_troubleshooting.md](setup_troubleshooting.md) instead â€” that file owns the setup surface and this one does not duplicate it.
5	
6	Spyglass **does not** wrap DataJoint errors: `SpyglassMixin` and `PopulateMixin` pass exceptions through unchanged (`src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:118`). The only Spyglass-specific exception class is a bare `PopulateException` in `src/spyglass/common/errors.py`. So the traceback you see *is* the DataJoint traceback, and the root cause is almost never the final line.
7	
8	## Contents
9	
10	- [Error-signature index](#error-signature-index)
11	- [When to use this file](#when-to-use-this-file)
12	- [Required inputs](#required-inputs)
13	- [Core philosophy](#core-philosophy)
14	- [Procedure](#procedure)
15	- [Failure signatures](#failure-signatures)
16	  - [A. fetch1() cardinality](#a-fetch1-cardinality)
17	  - [B. Ambiguous truth value of an array](#b-ambiguous-truth-value-of-an-array)
18	  - [C. Silent row multiplication from joins](#c-silent-row-multiplication-from-joins)
19	  - [D. Special-case key failures](#d-special-case-key-failures)
20	  - [E. Transaction / reservation confusion](#e-transaction--reservation-confusion)
21	  - [F. Interval / epoch mismatch across pipelines](#f-interval--epoch-mismatch-across-pipelines)
22	  - [G. `populate(key)` with a non-PK dict iterates the whole Selection](#g-populatekey-with-a-non-pk-dict-iterates-the-whole-selection)
23	  - [H. IntegrityError on insert often means an ancestor row is missing](#h-integrityerror-on-insert-often-means-an-ancestor-row-is-missing)
24	- [Debugging `populate_all_common`](#debugging-populate_all_common)
25	- [Automatic heuristics](#automatic-heuristics)
26	- [Sub-modes](#sub-modes)
27	- [Output shape](#output-shape)
28	- [Cross-references](#cross-references)
29	
30	## Error-signature index
31	
32	Match the first informative line of your traceback against the left column; jump to the named signature. Use this before reading the full signature prose â€” it takes a 500-line file and turns it into a grep.
33	
34	| Traceback fingerprint | Signature |
35	| --- | --- |
36	| `DataJointError: fetch1 should only be called on relations with exactly one tuple` / `... no tuples` | [A. fetch1() cardinality](#a-fetch1-cardinality) |
37	| `ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()` | [B. Ambiguous truth value of an array](#b-ambiguous-truth-value-of-an-array) |
38	| Restriction returns more rows than expected, or a `fetch1()` that previously worked starts raising on the same key | [C. Silent row multiplication from joins](#c-silent-row-multiplication-from-joins) |
39	| `populate()` succeeds on most sessions, fails on one with `KeyError` / `AttributeError` on a `None` field / shape mismatch | [D. Special-case key failures](#d-special-case-key-failures) |
40	| "Job already reserved", worker won't retry; parallel populate crashes without naming the key | [E. Transaction / reservation confusion](#e-transaction--reservation-confusion) |
41	| `populate()` silently completes with no new rows; a cross-pipeline join returns zero rows despite each side having data | [F. Interval / epoch mismatch across pipelines](#f-interval--epoch-mismatch-across-pipelines) |
42	| `Exception: The sorter kilosort2 is not installed` (or other wrong-sorter / wrong-params) when you asked for something else | [G. populate(key) with a non-PK dict iterates the whole Selection](#g-populatekey-with-a-non-pk-dict-iterates-the-whole-selection) |
43	| `IntegrityError: Cannot add or update a child row: a foreign key constraint fails` | [H. IntegrityError on insert often means an ancestor row is missing](#h-integrityerror-on-insert-often-means-an-ancestor-row-is-missing) |
44	| Idle hang, no CPU progress, no log output â€” populate just sits there | [I. populate() or a query hangs indefinitely](#i-populate-or-a-query-hangs-indefinitely) |
45	| `ValueError: Could not find exactly 1 datajoint user <name> in common.LabMember.LabMemberInfo` | (not here â€” go to [setup_troubleshooting.md Â§ AccessError / PermissionError on a shared installation](setup_troubleshooting.md#accesserror--permissionerror-on-a-shared-installation)) |
46	| `Could not find SPYGLASS_BASE_DIR`, connection refused, import failure | (not here â€” go to [setup_troubleshooting.md](setup_troubleshooting.md); that file owns the setup surface) |
47	
48	If no fingerprint matches, read [When to use this file](#when-to-use-this-file) and the procedure below; otherwise the [Automatic heuristics](#automatic-heuristics) at the end of this file catch the most common untagged shapes.
49	
50	## When to use this file
51	
52	Route here when any of the following hold:
53	
54	- `populate()` raises, but you cannot tell which key failed
55	- `make()` succeeds on most keys and fails on one
56	- A restriction returns too many or too few rows and `fetch1()` explodes
57	- A join unexpectedly duplicates or drops rows
58	- DataJoint internals dominate the traceback but the real cause is unclear
59	- The table populates successfully but the stored result looks wrong
60	- A comparison involving a NumPy array, pandas Series, or DataFrame breaks inside `make()` (typically `The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous`)
61	- A parallel populate hides which worker / key caused the crash
62	
63	If the user's problem is **install, config, Docker, MySQL, imports, Apple Silicon, or disk space**, route to [setup_troubleshooting.md](setup_troubleshooting.md) â€” do not handle it here.
64	
65	## Required inputs
66	
67	Before proposing anything, ask for or infer:
68	
69	- Full traceback (not just the final line)
70	- The table name that failed
71	- The exact `.populate(...)` or `.make(...)` call, including any restriction
72	- The table definition (primary key + dependencies)
73	- The relevant portion of `make()` when it's user code
74	- The failing key, if known
75	- A one-sentence statement of what the code was supposed to do
76	
77	Also helpful when available:
78	
79	- `key_source` for the failing table (what drives `populate()` iteration)
80	- Upstream table definitions referenced inside `make()`
81	- One key that succeeds and one that fails â€” the comparison is usually decisive
82	- Shape / dtype / type printouts of important intermediate variables
83	
84	If the user pastes only the final traceback line, ask for the full traceback before guessing. The real cause is typically several frames above the line they pasted.
85	
86	**Evidence shortcuts.** Before asking the user to paste table headings, parents, or row counts, try the bundled scripts: `python skills/spyglass/scripts/db_graph.py describe Cls --count --json` returns the runtime heading + parent / child / part adjacency + row count in one call; `python skills/spyglass/scripts/db_graph.py find-instance --class Cls --key f=v --count --json` confirms whether a specific key exists or how many rows match a partial restriction. Both run read-only against the live DB and emit JSON the user can paste back. For source-only declarations (FK definition, mixin-inherited methods), `python skills/spyglass/scripts/code_graph.py describe Cls --json` is faster and needs no DB.
87	
88	## Core philosophy
89	
90	Always assume:
91	
92	1. **The final traceback line may not be the true cause.** DataJoint internals routinely surface at the bottom of the stack when the bug is in user code several frames up.
93	2. **One bad key is more informative than the whole populate call.** Reproduce on a single key before rewriting anything.
94	3. **Relational assumptions break as often as Python code does.** Many "DataJoint bugs" are really cardinality bugs â€” the restriction or join returned a different row count than the code assumed.
95	4. **Scientific Python objects are frequent hidden causes.** Array equality, pandas truthiness, dtype surprises, and shape mismatches often masquerade as DataJoint errors.
96	5. **The smallest useful diagnostic beats a speculative rewrite.** Prefer a 2-line print over a 50-line refactor until the root cause is confirmed.
97	
98	## Procedure
99	
100	Work in this order. Don't skip to step 6.
101	
102	### Step 1 â€” Classify the failure layer
103	
104	Decide which of these four layers is actually failing:
105	
106	- **Layer A â€” DataJoint orchestration**: `populate()` reservation, `use_transaction`, parallel workers. Symptoms: worker died silently, job reserved but not running, transaction rolled back.
107	- **Layer B â€” Relational logic**: keys, restrictions, joins, merge-table resolution, cardinality. Symptoms: `fetch1()` raises on 0 or >1 rows, joins duplicate results, `merge_get_part()` returns the wrong partition.
108	- **Layer C â€” User code in make()**: the Python/NumPy/pandas work inside the table's `make()`. Symptoms: `ValueError`, `KeyError`, shape mismatches, `ambiguous truth value`.
109	- **Layer D â€” Spyglass wrapper behavior**: merge-table classmethod dispatch, interval helpers, `_Merge` mechanics. Symptoms: classmethod calls silently dropping restrictions, merge resolution returning unexpected part tables. See [merge_methods.md](merge_methods.md).
110	
111	State which layer is most likely before proposing anything.
112	
113	### Step 2 â€” Identify the failing key
114	
115	This is the single highest-leverage step. Spyglass ships no `debug_populate` or `populate_one` helper, so the pattern is manual:
116	
117	```python
118	# Enumerate the keys the failing table would process
119	keys = (MyTable.key_source & restriction).fetch("KEY")
120	print(f"{len(keys)} keys to process")
121	
122	# Run them one at a time so tracebacks name the failing key
123	for k in keys:
124	    try:
125	        MyTable().populate(k, reserve_jobs=False)
126	    except Exception as e:
127	        print(f"FAILED on key: {k}")
128	        print(f"  {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
129	        break   # or continue, if you want to see all failures
130	```
131	
132	Ask:
133	
134	- Does the error happen on every key, or only one?
135	- Can the failing key be reproduced with `MyTable().make(failing_key)` directly (bypassing `populate()`)?
136	- How does the failing key differ from one that succeeds? Different interval, subject, session length, sampling rate, electrode count?
137	
138	Once you have a specific failing key, the rest of the procedure becomes concrete.
139	
140	### Step 3 â€” Check relational assumptions
141	
142	For each fetch / join / restriction touched by the failing code:
143	
144	- What primary-key fields uniquely identify a row in this relation?
145	- Should the restriction return exactly 0, 1, or many rows? What does it actually return?
146	- After a join, did the row count change? Was that expected?
147	- Is `fetch1()` logically valid here â€” i.e., does the code *know* there's exactly one row?
148	- Are there hidden duplicates upstream (e.g., multiple parameter sets, multiple pipeline versions for the same session)?
149	- Is a merge table introducing multiplicity via its parts? (If you restrict a merge table with a friendly key like `{"nwb_file_name": ...}`, you typically get every part-table entry for that session.)
150	
151	The single question that catches the most bugs:
152	
153	> Does this relation have the cardinality the next line of code assumes?
154	
155	See [datajoint_api.md](datajoint_api.md#restriction-) for restriction/join semantics and the too-loose-restriction footgun.
156	
157	### Step 4 â€” Check object and type assumptions in make()
158	
159	Many "DataJoint errors" during `make()` are scientific-code errors. Look for:
160	
161	- `arr1 == arr2` used in a boolean context (`if`, `and`, `or`) where one side is an array
162	- `if series:` or `if df:` on a pandas object
163	- `object` dtype arrays â€” almost always a sign of ragged data sneaking in
164	- Shape mismatches between keys (one session has 4 LEDs, another has 2)
165	- Empty intervals, empty spike trains, or NaN-only epochs
166	- Assumptions that a fetched array has fixed length across keys
167	
168	Print `type(obj)`, `obj.shape`, and `obj.dtype` before the failing line. Ninety percent of the time the type is not what you expected.
169	
170	### Step 5 â€” Propose 2â€“5 narrow confirmation checks
171	
172	Do **not** jump to a rewrite. Propose the smallest diagnostics that would confirm or refute your hypothesis. Good ones:
173	
174	```python
175	# Cardinality check before fetch1
176	rel = (UpstreamTable & key)
177	print(f"rel has {len(rel)} rows; primary key fields = {rel.primary_key}")
178	
179	# Type/shape check before the failing line
180	print(f"type={type(x).__name__}, shape={getattr(x, 'shape', None)}, dtype={getattr(x, 'dtype', None)}")
181	
182	# Compare failing and succeeding keys
183	for k in [succeeding_key, failing_key]:
184	    r = (UpstreamTable & k).fetch()
185	    print(k, "->", len(r), r.dtype.names)
186	```
187	
188	Each check should be 1â€“3 lines and run in under a second.
189	
190	### Step 6 â€” Minimal fix
191	
192	Give the narrowest repair that addresses the confirmed cause. Examples:
193	
194	- Replace `arr1 == arr2` with `np.array_equal(arr1, arr2)` in a boolean context
195	- Replace `fetch1()` with `fetch()` plus an explicit row-count assertion
196	- Tighten a restriction so it returns exactly one row
197	- Coerce a pandas object to a NumPy array before comparison
198	- Guard against empty relations with an early return
199	- Handle the one special-case key explicitly
200	
201	Do not bundle a refactor with the fix. Keep them separate.
202	
203	### Step 7 â€” Robust fix
204	
205	Only after the minimal fix lands, describe the more durable solution:
206	
207	- Add a shape/type assertion at the top of `make()` so the next mismatch fails loudly
208	- Check `len(relation) == 1` before every `fetch1()` and raise with a descriptive message
209	- Validate key cardinality before joining upstream relations
210	- Standardize object types at the table boundary (always NumPy, always float64, etc.)
211	- Split long `make()` bodies into helpers with explicit pre/post invariants
212	- Add debug logging around the failing key category
213	
214	The minimal fix unsticks the user. The robust fix stops the same class of bug from returning.
215	
216	## Failure signatures
217	
218	Each signature follows the same shape so the triage output is consistent.
219	
220	### A. fetch1() cardinality
221	
222	**Symptom.** `DataJointError: fetch1 should only be called on relations with exactly one tuple` (or `no tuples`). Sometimes surfaces as `ValueError` from wrappers like `merge_get_part()` or `fetch1_dataframe()`. **Decoding-specific variant** â€” `DecodingOutput.fetch_results()` does NOT call `fetch1()`; it routes through `merge_restrict_class` (`utils/dj_merge_tables.py:770`), which raises `ValueError: Ambiguous entry. Data has mult rows in parent: ...` for the same diagnostic shape (under-specified restriction â†’ multiple parent matches). Different error class, same fix.
223	
224	**Most likely root cause.** The restriction in front of `fetch1()` (or `merge_restrict_class`) is either too loose (matches multiple rows â€” every interval, every parameter set, every pipeline version) or too tight (matches zero rows because a field was wrong).
225	
226	**Why that explanation fits.** `fetch1()` is defined to raise on anything other than exactly one row, and Spyglass's universal wrappers `merge_get_part` and `fetch1_dataframe` call it internally. `DecodingOutput.fetch_results` instead routes through `merge_restrict_class`, which has its own multi-row guard and raises `ValueError` (`utils/dj_merge_tables.py:782-786`).
227	
228	**Fastest confirmation checks.**
229	
230	```python
231	rel = (SomeTable & key)
232	print(len(rel))              # should be exactly 1
233	rel.fetch(as_dict=True)      # shows every matching row and its full primary key
234	```
235	
236	**Minimal fix.** Add the missing primary-key fields to the restriction so it uniquely identifies one row:
237	
238	```python
239	# Before (under-specified)
240	key = {"nwb_file_name": nwb_file}
241	(SomeTable & key).fetch1()
242	
243	# After (fully specified)
244	key = {"nwb_file_name": nwb_file,
245	       "interval_list_name": "02_r1",
246	       "trodes_pos_params_name": "default"}
247	(SomeTable & key).fetch1()
248	```
249	
250	**Robust fix.** Wrap `fetch1()` patterns in a small helper that validates `len(rel) == 1` with a message naming the fields the caller passed, so the next too-loose restriction fails with a pinpoint error.
251	
252	**Watch-outs.** `fetch_nwb()` does **not** raise on multiple rows â€” it silently returns a list across every match. If your code does `(Table & key).fetch_nwb()[0]` on an under-specified restriction, you get a plausibly-shaped result from an arbitrary row. See [datajoint_api.md](datajoint_api.md#fetch-fetch--fetch1) for the detailed footgun.
253	
254	### B. Ambiguous truth value of an array
255	
256	**Symptom.** `ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()` â€” typically inside `make()`, during an `if`, `and`, `or`, or comparison.
257	
258	**Most likely root cause.** A NumPy array or pandas Series ended up somewhere the code expects a scalar boolean.
259	
260	**Why that explanation fits.** NumPy deliberately raises on `bool(array)` when the array has more than one element; pandas does the same on Series/DataFrame in most contexts.
261	
262	**Fastest confirmation checks.**
263	
264	```python
265	print(type(x).__name__, getattr(x, "shape", None), getattr(x, "dtype", None))
266	# If x is an ndarray or Series, any `if x:` or `x == y` inside `if` will trip this.
267	```
268	
269	**Minimal fix.** Pick the operator that matches the intent:
270	
271	- Element-wise equality with a scalar boolean result â†’ `np.array_equal(a, b)`
272	- "Is any element true" â†’ `arr.any()`
273	- "Are all elements true" â†’ `arr.all()`
274	- pandas object equality â†’ `a.equals(b)`
275	
276	**Robust fix.** At the boundary of `make()`, coerce inputs to a known type (`np.asarray(x, dtype=float)`) so downstream comparisons are always array-vs-array with documented shape, not "some scalar / some array depending on the key."
277	
278	**Watch-outs.** Same family includes `KeyError` inside a DataFrame boolean indexer, and `IndexError` when a scalar index was expected but an array arrived. Print `type(...)` before indexing.
279	
280	### C. Silent row multiplication from joins
281	
282	**Symptom.** Results have more rows than expected, `fetch1()` starts raising on a restriction that used to work, or a populated table contains more entries than the number of source sessions.
283	
284	**Most likely root cause.** A relation you assumed was one-to-one is actually one-to-many. Common culprits: merge tables (one `nwb_file_name` â†’ many parts), parameter tables (one session â†’ many parameter sets), interval lists (one session â†’ many intervals).
285	
286	**Why that explanation fits.** DataJoint joins are natural joins over shared primary-key fields; any field that looks like a foreign key but is actually repeated across the upstream table multiplies rows.
287	
288	**Fastest confirmation checks.**
289	
290	```python
291	# Count before and after the join
292	print(len(UpstreamA & key))                # e.g., 1
293	print(len(UpstreamB & key))                # e.g., 7
294	print(len((UpstreamA * UpstreamB) & key))  # if 7, the join multiplied as expected
295	# Inspect the join keys
296	set(UpstreamA.heading.names) & set(UpstreamB.heading.names)
297	```
298	
299	**Minimal fix.** Tighten the restriction so the join side that multiplies is reduced to one row first:
300	
301	```python
302	one_param = (TrodesPosParams & {"trodes_pos_params_name": "default"}).fetch1("KEY")
303	(UpstreamA & key) * (UpstreamB & one_param)
304	```
305	
306	Or aggregate before joining with `.aggr()` if you truly want a many-to-one rollup.
307	
308	**Robust fix.** In `make()`, check cardinality before fetching:
309	
310	```python
311	n_rows = len(rel)
312	if n_rows != 1:
313	    raise ValueError(
314	        f"{rel.heading.primary_key} matched {n_rows} rows for key={key}"
315	    )
316	```
317	
318	This catches multiplicity at the earliest possible point rather than at a confusing `fetch1()` further down.
319	
320	**Watch-outs.** Restricting a merge table with a friendly key (e.g., `{"nwb_file_name": ...}`) almost always multiplies â€” use `merge_get_part()` or `merge_restrict()` instead. See [merge_methods.md](merge_methods.md).
321	
322	### D. Special-case key failures
323	
324	**Symptom.** `populate()` runs fine for most sessions and crashes on one. Error is often a shape mismatch, `KeyError`, or an `AttributeError` on a field that is `None` only for the failing session.
325	
326	**Most likely root cause.** `make()` assumes all keys have the same data shape (same number of LEDs, non-empty position array, non-NaN intervals, a specific epoch label) but one session violates the assumption.
327	
328	**Why that explanation fits.** Science data is messy. Different recording rigs, different animals, different experimenter conventions â†’ one session per batch that breaks the invariants.
329	
330	**Fastest confirmation checks.** Compare a succeeding key to the failing key side-by-side:
331	
332	```python
333	for k in [good_key, bad_key]:
334	    upstream = (UpstreamTable & k).fetch1()
335	    print(k["nwb_file_name"],
336	          "shape=", upstream["data"].shape,
337	          "dtype=", upstream["data"].dtype,
338	          "nan_frac=", np.isnan(upstream["data"]).mean())
339	```
340	
341	Pay attention to the first field that differs â€” that's almost always the cause.
342	
343	**Minimal fix.** Add an explicit guard in `make()` for the special case (skip, pad, raise with a descriptive message), or upstream-validate the bad session out of `key_source`.
344	
345	**Robust fix.** Move the invariant check to the top of `make()` with a clear error that names the offending field; if the pattern recurs across pipelines, factor the validator into a helper. Consider tightening the upstream `SelectionTable` so the bad session never reaches `populate()`.
346	
347	**Watch-outs.** "Works on my machine" often means "the failing key isn't in your local test subset." Before declaring the fix done, run the single failing key through `make()` directly.
348	
349	### E. Transaction / reservation confusion
350	
351	**Symptom.** `populate()` fails repeatedly without naming the failing key; subsequent runs say the job is reserved; parallel populate hides which worker crashed.
352	
353	**Most likely root cause.** DataJoint's orchestration (reservation, transactions, parallel workers) is masking the underlying error from steps 2â€“4. Spyglass doesn't customize this â€” `PopulateMixin` delegates to DataJoint's `populate()` (`src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:98`), and a single worker failure in `NonDaemonPool` kills the entire pool.
354	
355	**Why that explanation fits.** With `reserve_jobs=True`, failed keys are written to the `~jobs` table and skipped on the next call; with `use_transaction=True` (Spyglass default), the failing row is rolled back so post-mortem inspection shows no partial state; with parallel workers, only the first exception propagates.
356	
357	**Fastest confirmation checks.** Drop orchestration and reproduce serially:
358	
359	```python
360	# Single key, no reservation, no parallelism
361	MyTable().populate(
362	    failing_key,
363	    reserve_jobs=False,
364	    processes=1,
365	)
366	# Or bypass populate entirely
367	MyTable().make(failing_key)
368	```
369	
370	Inspect the jobs table to see reserved/errored keys. **Derive the
371	jobs table from the failing table** rather than hard-coding a
372	schema name â€” Spyglass schemas are split across many database names
373	(`common`, `lfp_v1`, `spikesorting_v1`, `position_v1`, `decoding_v1`,
374	etc.; a quick check is the `database` attribute on the failing
375	table's connection). Each schema has its own `~jobs` table:
376	
377	```python
378	import datajoint as dj
379	
380	# Pick the schema that owns the table you're populating. `MyTable.database`
381	# is the underlying schema name; use it instead of guessing.
382	schema_name = MyTable.database
383	jobs = dj.Schema(schema_name).jobs
384	
385	# Filter to this table's failed entries before doing anything:
386	errors = jobs & {
387	    "table_name": MyTable.table_name,
388	    "status": "error",
389	}
390	errors.fetch(as_dict=True)
391	errors.delete_quick()   # only after confirming you want to re-run those keys
392	```
393	
394	**Minimal fix.** Debug with orchestration off. Once the true cause is fixed, re-enable reservation/parallelism for the full run.
395	
396	**Robust fix.** For pipelines that routinely hit this, document the "debug single key" idiom in the pipeline's README and wrap common diagnostic calls in a small helper. If `use_transaction=False` is required (for long-running populates with external file writes), know that Spyglass adds an upstream-hash check around it (`src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:88-108`) that will raise if an upstream table changes mid-populate.
397	
398	**Watch-outs.** A reserved job from a previous crashed run will silently skip in subsequent populates and look like "nothing is happening." Always check `jobs` before concluding that `populate()` is broken.
399	
400	### F. Interval / epoch mismatch across pipelines
401	
402	**Symptom.** Upstream tables look populated, restrictions look sensible, but the downstream output is empty, suspiciously small, or the populate silently does nothing. Joining two upstream tables returns zero rows even though each has rows for the session.
403	
404	**Most likely root cause.** Different pipelines take different interval-name fields as input, and a selection was made against one interval while a downstream step expected another. Spyglass does **not** use a single universal `target_interval_list_name` â€” pipelines name their interval selection differently, and some pipelines take *two* intervals that can differ from each other.
405	
406	Field names vary across the codebase. A non-exhaustive map:
407	
408	| Pipeline / table | Interval field in its selection |
409	|---|---|
410	| `IntervalList` (the source) | `interval_list_name` (primary key, `common_interval.py:28`) |
411	| `LFPSelection` / `LFPV1` | `target_interval_list_name` (`lfp/v1/lfp.py`) |
412	| `LFPArtifactRemovedIntervalList` | `artifact_removed_interval_list_name` (`lfp/v1/lfp_artifact.py:162`) |
413	| `LFPBandSelection` | `target_interval_list_name` (`lfp/analysis/v1/lfp_band.py:28`) |
414	| `SpikeSortingRecordingSelection` (v0) | `sort_interval_name` â€” inherited via `-> SortInterval` (field defined in `SortInterval` at `:241`; declared on `SpikeSortingRecordingSelection` at `spikesorting_recording.py:324-332`) |
415	| `SpikeSortingArtifactDetectionSelection` | `artifact_removed_interval_list_name` (`spikesorting/v0/spikesorting_artifact.py:88`) |
416	| `RippleLFPSelection` (feeds `RippleTimesV1`) | `target_interval_list_name` â€” **inherited two hops** via `-> LFPBandV1 -> LFPBandSelection`. `RippleLFPSelection` (`ripple/v1/ripple.py:33-37`) has no interval field of its own; the inherited name only shows up in the transitive primary key, so check `RippleLFPSelection.heading.primary_key` to see it. |
417	| `MuaEventsV1` | `detection_interval` (projected from `IntervalList.interval_list_name` at `mua/v1/mua.py:68`) |
418	| Decoding V1 selections | `encoding_interval` AND `decoding_interval` (both projected from `IntervalList.interval_list_name`, `decoding/v1/clusterless.py:88-89`) |
419	| `TrodesPosSelection` | `interval_list_name` (unaliased â€” inherited via `-> RawPosition -> IntervalList`) |
420	| DLC position selections | Interval is not set at the user-facing selection level; it flows in through the pose-estimation chain. Inspect the specific selection's primary key to confirm. |
421	
422	Two intervals can overlap in time but live under different names (e.g., one session's raw epoch vs. a trimmed "valid_times" version; a position-computed interval vs. the raw recording interval). A restriction that works on the LFP selection table may match zero rows on the decoding selection table because the field name, the interval name, or both differ.
423	
424	**Why that explanation fits.** DataJoint silently ignores restriction fields that aren't present on the table (the field is treated as a no-op), and silently produces empty joins when key fields match on name but not on value. Both shapes look "correct" to the user but return nothing.
425	
426	**Fastest confirmation checks.**
427	
428	```python
429	# List all intervals actually defined for this session
430	(IntervalList & {"nwb_file_name": f}).fetch("interval_list_name")
431	
432	# Inspect the selection table's interval field(s) directly
433	print(UpstreamSelection.heading.primary_key)  # shows the exact field names
434	(UpstreamSelection & {"nwb_file_name": f}).fetch(as_dict=True, limit=5)
435	
436	# If a downstream populate is empty, check key_source:
437	print(len(DownstreamTable.key_source & key))  # 0 = no upstream rows match
438	
439	# For two-interval pipelines (decoding), check BOTH explicitly:
440	# encoding_interval and decoding_interval may differ â€” and they often should,
441	# but not by accident.
442	```
443	
444	**Minimal fix.** Align the interval name(s) with what the downstream table's selection expects. Either (a) re-insert the downstream selection using the correct interval-name value, or (b) if the intervals really are different epochs and need intersection, compute the intersection explicitly using `IntervalList.fetch1("valid_times")` and build a new `IntervalList` row for the intersected span.
445	
446	**Robust fix.** In custom pipelines, project the upstream interval field onto the downstream selection so the name travels with the data instead of being re-specified (`-> IntervalList.proj(my_interval='interval_list_name')`, matching the decoding V1 pattern). When the name is a projection you can't accidentally typo it on the downstream side.
447	
448	**Watch-outs.**
449	
450	- **Empty populate â‰  error.** DataJoint's `populate()` silently does nothing when `key_source & key` is empty. See the pre-populate feedback loop in [feedback_loops.md](feedback_loops.md) â€” a `len(key_source & key) > 0` assertion before populate catches this immediately.
451	- **Name equality â‰  time equality.** `"02_r1"` in one table and `"02_r1"` in another probably refer to the same IntervalList row (same PK), but `"02_r1_valid"` is a different row even if its `valid_times` overlap. Check by joining on the `IntervalList` PK when in doubt.
452	- **Decoding specifically.** `encoding_interval` and `decoding_interval` are intentional separate inputs. They CAN be the same; they often SHOULDN'T be (train on encoding, evaluate on decoding). Verify by design intent, not by assuming they're equal.
453	
454	### G. `populate(key)` with a non-PK dict iterates the whole Selection
455	
456	Unlike `fetch1()`, `populate(key)` does NOT raise on a loose dict.
457	Passing `{'nwb_file_name': ..., 'sorter': 'mountainsort4'}` to
458	`SpikeSorting.populate` silently walks every unprocessed row in the
459	Selection table â€” which may include other users' keys, different
460	sorters, or wrong sessions. Example of the resulting confusion:
461	
462	```
463	Exception: The sorter kilosort2 is not installed
464	# ...but I asked for mountainsort4!
465	```
466	
467	**Mechanism.** `populate(restrictions)` restricts against `key_source`,
468	which for a `dj.Computed` table defaults to each parent table projected
469	to its **primary key only** (DataJoint `autopopulate.py`, `key_source`
470	property: `else table.proj()`). For `SpikeSorting`,
471	`key_source = SpikeSortingSelection.proj()` â€” heading is just
472	`{sorting_id}`. DataJoint's `&` silently drops dict keys not in the
473	heading, so `sorter` and `nwb_file_name` are discarded and the
474	restriction becomes a no-op. The full Selection table's heading *does*
475	include those secondary attrs (from its `-> IntervalList` and
476	`-> SpikeSorterParameters` FKs), so a restriction on the Selection
477	directly works â€” but `populate()` doesn't use that heading, it uses
478	`key_source`'s.
479	
480	**Fix.** Restrict against the full Selection table for **the table you're
481	populating**, then project to a PK-only key before `populate`. For
482	`SpikeSorting.populate`, that's `SpikeSortingSelection`
483	(`spikesorting/v1/sorting.py:199`; PK = `sorting_id`):
484	
485	```python
486	# SpikeSorting.populate â€” fix the symptom-table's Selection (NOT
487	# SpikeSortingRecordingSelection, which gates the upstream
488	# `SpikeSortingRecording.populate` instead):
489	sort_key = (SpikeSortingSelection & key).fetch1('KEY')
490	SpikeSorting.populate(sort_key)
491	
492	# Multi-row populate:
493	SpikeSorting.populate((SpikeSortingSelection & key).proj())
494	
495	# Same shape one tier upstream â€” for SpikeSortingRecording.populate,
496	# fix SpikeSortingRecordingSelection:
497	rec_key = (SpikeSortingRecordingSelection & key).fetch1('KEY')
498	SpikeSortingRecording.populate(rec_key)
499	```
500	
501	Applies to every `*V1.populate(...)` entry point
502	(`ArtifactDetection`, `SpikeSorting`, `MetricCuration`,
503	`FigURLCuration`, etc.) and to any custom pipeline whose Selection
504	table primary key doesn't include the fields you naturally restrict by.
505	The key_source-PK-only behavior is the `dj.Computed` default, not
506	Spyglass-specific â€” override `key_source` on your own Computed table if
507	you want secondary attrs to be filterable directly.
508	
509	### H. IntegrityError on insert often means an ancestor row is missing
510	
511	`IntegrityError: Cannot add or update a child row: a foreign key
512	constraint fails` on a `*Selection` or analysis-table insert usually
513	means one of the primary-key fields in the insert dict has no matching
514	row in an ancestor table, *not* in the table you're inserting into.
515	
516	**Find the missing upstream:**
517	
518	```python
519	# SpyglassMixin ships a diagnostic helper. It RETURNS the diagnostic

532	### I. `populate()` or a query hangs indefinitely
533	
534	Long idle stalls (no CPU, no progress) usually mean **lock contention** â€” another worker or an abandoned transaction is holding a MySQL lock your call is waiting on, not a slow `make()` body. First rule out "the DB isn't reachable at all" with `python skills/spyglass/scripts/verify_spyglass_env.py --check dj_connection --timeout 10` â€” `check_threads` itself needs a live connection and will hang the same way if the server's unreachable. Once connectivity is confirmed, diagnose with `AnyTable().check_threads(detailed=True)` (any `SpyglassMixin` table works); it returns a DataFrame of live threads from `performance_schema` including blockers. Coordinate with the lab before killing an abandoned transaction.
535	
536	If a `.fetch()` or `.fetch1()` call hangs with no CPU activity â€” not slow compute, just an *idle* long fetch â€” go straight to `check_threads(detailed=True)`. User-perceived "this fetch is taking forever" is almost always lock contention, not query plan.
537	
538	## Debugging `populate_all_common`
539	
540	`populate_all_common` swallows per-table exceptions by default (`raise_err=False`), logging only a short message to `common_usage.InsertError`. When a fresh ingest "completes" but common tables silently miss rows, that's the usual cause. The fix is small (`raise_err=True` or populate tables directly) but the diagnostic context is enough that it lives in its own reference: see [populate_all_common_debugging.md](populate_all_common_debugging.md).
541	
542	## Automatic heuristics
543	
544	Apply these before asking the user:
545	
546	1. **Traceback ends in DataJoint internals â†’ suspect `make()` first.** The last frame is the framework; the bug is usually in user code several frames up.
547	2. **Only some keys fail â†’ compare a succeeding key to a failing key before editing code.** This narrows the cause faster than reading `make()` line by line.
548	3. **`fetch1()` is involved â†’ verify relation cardinality explicitly** with `len(rel)` before proposing a fix.
549	4. **NumPy or pandas objects near the failure â†’ print `type`, `shape`, `dtype`** before hypothesizing.
550	5. **Spyglass interval logic is involved â†’ verify the restriction returns the intended epoch/interval rows** with `(IntervalList & restriction).fetch("interval_list_name")`.
551	
552	## Sub-modes
553	
554	Same reference, three entry points depending on what the user brought:
555	
556	- **Traceback-first.** Input is an error log. Start at step 1 (classify layer), then skip to the matching [failure signature](#failure-signatures).
557	- **Relational-logic-first.** Code runs but returns wrong rows. Skip to step 3 (cardinality) and signatures A and C.
558	- **Failing-key comparison.** One key fails, another succeeds. Skip to step 2 then step 4, and signature D.
559	
560	## Output shape
561	
562	When responding to a runtime-debug question, structure the reply as:
563	
564	1. **Symptom** â€” one line restating what failed.
565	2. **Most likely root cause** â€” your current best explanation, stated as a claim you're prepared to verify.
566	3. **Why that explanation fits** â€” what in the evidence points here rather than to alternatives.
567	4. **Fastest confirmation checks** â€” 2â€“5 narrow diagnostics, each 1â€“3 lines of code.
568	5. **Minimal fix** â€” narrowest repair that addresses the confirmed cause.
569	6. **Robust fix** â€” the durable change that prevents the class of bug from returning.
570	7. **Watch-outs** â€” adjacent failure modes the user should still keep in mind.
571	
572	Keep steps 4â€“7 terse. The confirmation checks are the part that matters; the fixes follow once the checks land.
573	
574	## Cross-references

576	- [datajoint_api.md](datajoint_api.md) â€” restriction/join semantics, `fetch1()` cardinality footgun, too-loose-restriction pattern
577	- [merge_methods.md](merge_methods.md) â€” merge-table classmethod dispatch gotcha, `_Merge` methods, projected FK rename

3	from spyglass.utils.dj_helper_fn import NonDaemonPool, populate_pass_function
4	from spyglass.utils.mixins.base import BaseMixin
5	
6	
7	class PopulateMixin(BaseMixin):
8	
9	    _parallel_make = False  # Tables that use parallel processing in make
10	    _use_transaction = True  # Use transaction in populate.
11	
12	    # -------------------------------- populate --------------------------------
13	
14	    def _hash_upstream(self, keys):
15	        """Hash upstream table keys for no transaction populate.
16	
17	        Uses a RestrGraph to capture all upstream tables, restrict them to
18	        relevant entries, and hash the results. This is used to check if
19	        upstream tables have changed during a no-transaction populate and avoid
20	        the following data-integrity error:
21	
22	        1. User A starts no-transaction populate.
23	        2. User B deletes and repopulates an upstream table, changing contents.
24	        3. User A finishes populate, inserting data that is now invalid.
25	
26	        Parameters
27	        ----------
28	        keys : list
29	            List of keys for populating table.
30	        """
31	        RestrGraph = self._graph_deps[1]
32	        if not (parents := self.parents(as_objects=True, primary=True)):
33	            # Should not happen, as this is only called from populated tables
34	            raise RuntimeError("No upstream tables found for upstream hash.")
35	
36	        if isinstance(keys, dict):
37	            keys = [keys]  # case for single population key
38	        leaves = {  # Restriction on each primary parent
39	            p.full_table_name: [
40	                {k: v for k, v in key.items() if k in p.heading.names}
41	                for key in keys
42	            ]
43	            for p in parents
44	        }
45	
46	        return RestrGraph(seed_table=self, leaves=leaves, cascade=True).hash
47	
48	    def populate(self, *restrictions, **kwargs):
49	        """Populate table in parallel, with or without transaction protection.
50	
51	        Supersedes datajoint.table.Table.populate for classes with that
52	        spawn processes in their make function and always use transactions.
53	
54	        `_use_transaction` class attribute can be set to False to disable
55	        transaction protection for a table. This is not recommended for tables
56	        with short processing times. A before-and-after hash check is performed
57	        to ensure upstream tables have not changed during populate, and may
58	        be a more time-consuming process. To permit the `make` to insert without
59	        populate, set `_allow_insert` to True.
60	        """
61	        processes = kwargs.pop("processes", 1)
62	
63	        # Decide if using transaction protection
64	        use_transact = kwargs.pop("use_transaction", None)
65	        if use_transact is None:  # if user does not specify, use class default
66	            use_transact = self._use_transaction
67	            if self._use_transaction is False:  # To be deprecated #1422
68	                from spyglass.common.common_usage import ActivityLog
69	
70	                ActivityLog().deprecate_log(
71	                    "no_transaction_make", alt="tri-part make"
72	                )
73	
74	        if use_transact is False and processes > 1:
75	            raise RuntimeError(
76	                "Must use transaction protection with parallel processing.\n"
77	                + "Call with use_transation=True.\n"
78	                + f"Table default transaction use: {self._use_transaction}"
79	            )
80	
81	        # Get keys, needed for no-transact or multi-process w/_parallel_make
82	        keys = [True]
83	        if use_transact is False or (processes > 1 and self._parallel_make):
84	            keys = (self._jobs_to_do(restrictions) - self.target).fetch(
85	                "KEY", limit=kwargs.get("limit", None)
86	            )
87	
88	        if use_transact is False:
89	            upstream_hash = self._hash_upstream(keys)
90	            if kwargs:  # Warn of ignoring populate kwargs, bc using `make`
91	                self._logger.warning(
92	                    "Ignoring kwargs when not using transaction protection."
93	                )
94	
95	        if processes == 1 or not self._parallel_make:
96	            if use_transact:  # Pass single-process populate to super
97	                kwargs["processes"] = processes
98	                return super().populate(*restrictions, **kwargs)
99	            else:  # No transaction protection, use bare make
100	                for key in keys:
101	                    self.make(key)
102	                if upstream_hash != self._hash_upstream(keys):
103	                    (self & keys).delete(safemode=False)
104	                    self._logger.error(
105	                        "Upstream tables changed during non-transaction "
106	                        + "populate. Please try again."
107	                    )
108	                return None
109	
110	        # If parallel in both make and populate, use non-daemon processes
111	        # package the call list
112	        call_list = [(type(self), key, kwargs) for key in keys]
113	
114	        # Create a pool of non-daemon processes to populate a single entry each
115	        pool = NonDaemonPool(processes=processes)
116	        try:
117	            pool.map(populate_pass_function, call_list)
118	        except Exception as e:
119	            raise e
120	        finally:
121	            pool.close()
122	            pool.terminate()
123

# Isolating the failing key in a partially-completed `LFPV1.populate()`

Yes â€” you don't need to rerun anything one-at-a-time by hand. There are three legitimate, complementary ways to find the broken key, all from standard DataJoint. Spyglass ships **no** `debug_populate` / `populate_one` helper; `PopulateMixin.populate` just delegates to DataJoint's `populate()` (`src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:48-122`), so all the recovery levers below are stock-DataJoint facilities.

One important up-front fact: Spyglass's default is `_use_transaction = True` (`src/spyglass/utils/mixins/populate.py:10`). When a `make()` raises, DataJoint rolls the transaction back, so post-mortem you will not see partial residue in `LFPV1` for the failing key â€” its row never landed. That's good (no cleanup), but it means the only record of *what failed* is whatever you can recover from the jobs table or by re-running.

## Symptom

`LFPV1.populate()` ran on ~20 sessions, succeeded on most, failed on one (or a few), with a deep DataJoint traceback that doesn't name the offending `key`.

## Most likely root cause

A single key in `LFPV1.key_source` (which defaults to `LFPSelection.proj()` â€” `LFPV1` is `dj.Computed` with no custom `key_source` override) hit a data-shape or upstream-lookup problem inside `make()`. With transactions on, the failing row was rolled back; the parent `populate()` re-raised one exception from the loop and you saw frames from DataJoint's orchestration rather than the user-code frame.

## Three legitimate ways to identify the failing key

Pick whichever fits â€” they're not mutually exclusive.

### Option 1 â€” Manual try/except over `key_source`

Most explicit. Gives you a clean per-key traceback the moment something breaks:

```python
from spyglass.lfp.v1 import LFPV1

# Enumerate the keys LFPV1 would normally process
keys = LFPV1.key_source.fetch("KEY")
print(f"{len(keys)} keys in key_source")

failures = []
for k in keys:
    try:
        LFPV1().populate(k, reserve_jobs=False)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"FAILED on key: {k}")
        print(f"  {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
        failures.append((k, e))
        # `break` if you want to stop at the first one;
        # leave it to collect every failure in one pass.
```

Combine with `processes=1` (which is already the default in Spyglass's `populate` wrapper â€” see `populate.py:61`) so the traceback isn't filtered through a worker pool. Already-populated keys are skipped automatically; this won't redo work.

If you want a still-narrower repro once you have the key, bypass `populate()` entirely and call `make()` directly â€” it gives the most informative traceback because there's no orchestration in the way:

```python
LFPV1().make(failing_key)
```

### Option 2 â€” `suppress_errors=True, return_exception_objects=True`

Most efficient. One pass; DataJoint runs every remaining key, captures exceptions instead of raising, and returns a list of `(key, exception)` pairs:

```python
errors = LFPV1().populate(
    suppress_errors=True,
    return_exception_objects=True,
    reserve_jobs=False,
)
for key, exc in errors:
    print(key, "->", type(exc).__name__, exc)
```

This is the right shape when you want to see *all* failures in one shot rather than stopping at the first.

### Option 3 â€” Inspect the `~jobs` table

If the original overnight run used `reserve_jobs=True`, DataJoint has already recorded the failing key plus its traceback in the schema's `~jobs` table under `status='error'`. You don't need to rerun anything to find it:

```python
import datajoint as dj

# Derive the schema from the failing table â€” don't hard-code 'lfp_v1',
# because Spyglass schemas are split across many DB names.
schema_name = LFPV1.database              # the underlying schema
jobs = dj.Schema(schema_name).jobs

errors = jobs & {
    "table_name": LFPV1.table_name,       # the symptom table specifically
    "status": "error",
}
errors.fetch(as_dict=True)                # key + full traceback per failed row
```

Each row contains the failing primary key plus the captured traceback string, so you get the exception text without re-running. After you've fixed the cause, `errors.delete_quick()` clears the reservations so the next `populate()` retries them.

If your overnight run did *not* set `reserve_jobs=True`, this table will be empty for `LFPV1` â€” fall back to Option 1 or 2.

## Suggested order

1. Check `~jobs` first (Option 3). If the original run reserved jobs, the failing key and traceback are already there â€” zero re-run cost.
2. If `~jobs` has nothing useful, run Option 2 (`suppress_errors=True, return_exception_objects=True`) to enumerate every failure in one pass.
3. Once you have a specific failing key, reproduce it with `LFPV1().make(failing_key)` for the cleanest single-key traceback, then triage from there (compare it against a succeeding key â€” different interval, electrode count, NaN-only epoch, missing upstream row, etc.).

## Watch-outs

- **`use_transaction=True` is Spyglass's default** (`populate.py:10`). On failure, the partial row is rolled back, so don't expect to find half-written `LFPV1` entries for the broken key â€” they were never committed. That's why the jobs table or a re-run is the only post-mortem signal.
- **Don't reach for a Spyglass-specific debug helper** â€” there isn't one. `PopulateMixin.populate` just forwards to `super().populate(...)` when transactions are on (`populate.py:96-98`), so all standard DataJoint flags (`suppress_errors`, `return_exception_objects`, `reserve_jobs`, `processes`) work as documented.
- **Avoid `processes>1` while debugging.** Spyglass's wrapper defaults to `processes=1`; bumping it up hides which worker died. Single-process serial reruns give the cleanest traceback.
- **Reserved-but-not-cleared jobs** from a crashed run will silently skip on the next populate and look like "nothing is happening." If you adopt Option 3, remember to `delete_quick()` the error rows once the underlying bug is fixed, or future populates will keep skipping those keys.
